prompt: What are the LIABILITY and reputational barriers to a named human putting their signature on a third-party 'AI agent security readiness attestation' for a startup vendor? Research: professional/E&O (errors & omissions) insurance expectations for auditors and vCISOs, real cases of auditors facing liability for sign-offs, and what protections (scope-limiting language, indemnification, 'readiness not certification' framing, insurance) a co-signer would demand before lending their name to an Ed25519-signed report. Quote practitioners (@handle+date) or cite professional-body guidance. State the minimum legal/insurance scaffolding kolm must offer to recruit co-signers; cite each point.
content:
**Liability and reputational barriers for a named individual (e.g., auditor, vCISO, or security practitioner) signing a third-party “AI agent security readiness attestation” are substantial, primarily due to potential negligence claims, third-party reliance, evolving AI-specific risks, and professional standards.**[[1]](https://www.thehartford.com/professional-liability-insurance/auditors)[[2]](https://www.journalofaccountancy.com/issues/2013/may/20137570/)

Such attestations—especially cryptographically signed ones like Ed25519 reports—create a verifiable record that can be relied upon by clients, investors, insurers, regulators, or downstream users of the startup vendor’s AI agents. If vulnerabilities later emerge (common in fast-evolving AI/agent environments), signers face exposure even without formal audit standards.

### Professional/E&O Insurance Expectations
Auditors, CPAs, and vCISOs are routinely expected (or contractually required) to carry professional liability/errors & omissions (E&O/PLI) insurance. This covers claims of negligence, faulty advice, errors/omissions in professional services, and resulting financial harm.[[3]](https://www.vouch.us/blog/accounting-insurance)[[4]](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/e/errors-omissions-insurance.asp)

- **Typical requirements**: $1M–$2M per claim/occurrence and $2M+ aggregate (seen in vendor contracts, Cornell requirements for consultants, and vCISO service discussions). Higher limits (e.g., $5M+) may apply for complex or high-stakes work.[[5]](https://www.risk.cornell.edu/vendor-provider-main-page/consultant-or-non-specialist/)[[6]](https://www.cybervize.de/en/blog/virtual-ciso-costs-pricing-models)
- **vCISO specifics**: Coverage often needed for advisory liability, risk assessments, and compliance work. Employer policies (D&O or general E&O) frequently exclude or inadequately cover individual CISOs/vCISOs, who may be viewed as technical experts rather than officers. Personal policies, endorsements, or indemnification agreements are recommended. Cyber liability supplements are also common.[[7]](https://fortifyexperts.com/are-you-protected-what-insurance-cover-should-a-ciso-have/)[[8]](https://www.cfins.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/CISO-Professional-Liability-Insurance-11-24.pdf)[[9]](https://www.iansresearch.com/resources/all-blogs/post/security-blog/2025/01/16/how-to-mitigate-your-risk-as-a-ciso)
- Professional bodies and guidance emphasize risk management; e.g., ICAEW notes members should manage liability through agreements, hold-harmless letters, and clarifying work scope/purpose, especially for third-party reliance.[[10]](https://www.icaew.com/regulation/managing-professional-liability)

Without adequate coverage, personal assets are at risk.

### Real Cases of Auditor/Practitioner Liability
Auditors face claims from clients (breach of contract/negligence) and third parties (lenders, investors, banks) under common law, with liability varying by jurisdiction (e.g., *Ultramares v. Touche* (1931) generally limits ordinary negligence to “known” users but allows gross negligence/constructive fraud claims by unknown third parties).[[11]](https://www.ebsco.com/research-starters/history/ultramares-case-establishes-liability-auditors)

Notable examples include:
- SEC enforcement: In ~17% of financial misrepresentation cases (1996–2009 data), the signing audit firm/partner was also charged.[[12]](https://business.columbia.edu/sites/default/files-efs/pubfiles/13983/Kedia_Khan_Rajgopal_2018-JLFA.pdf)
- Third-party suits: Banks/lenders suing over undetected issues in audits (e.g., 2020 CNA claims data; specific cases involving multi-year audit failures leading to settlements).[[13]](https://foxforensicaccounting.com/blog/a-case-study-on-auditor-liability)
- Recent/high-profile: BDO faced arbitration award (~$9M) upheld in NY court for negligence in assessing hard-to-value assets (Platinum Partners hedge fund collapse; under appeal as of early 2026). Deloitte settled a $34M class action over audits tied to an abandoned nuclear project.[[14]](https://news.bloombergtax.com/financial-accounting/investors-give-courts-fresh-look-in-bid-to-hold-auditors-liable)
- Broader pattern: Claims often involve alleged failure to detect material issues; 22% of certain AICPA PL program claims in 2012 involved third parties.[[2]](https://www.journalofaccountancy.com/issues/2013/may/20137570/)

Reputational damage compounds this—public enforcement actions, settlements, or reports of “rubber-stamp” work erode credibility. AI-specific attestations amplify risk due to novel threats (e.g., agent drift, prompt injection) not covered by traditional controls.

### Protections Co-Signers Typically Demand
Before lending a name (especially to a cryptographically signed report), practitioners demand strong safeguards to limit exposure and clarify the non-audit nature of the work:

- **Scope-limiting language and disclaimers**: Explicit statements that the report is a limited “readiness attestation” (snapshot of specific controls/assumptions at a point in time), *not* a formal audit, examination, certification, or opinion under AICPA SSAE 18/attestation standards, PCAOB, or equivalent. It should disclaim guarantees of ongoing security, third-party reliance, or comprehensiveness.[[15]](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/sahar-dahan_audit-readiness-security-readiness-they-activity-7436902548340396032-cMsL)[[10]](https://www.icaew.com/regulation/managing-professional-liability)
- **“Readiness not certification” framing**: Distinguishes from binding certifications; emphasizes advisory/limited-scope nature to reduce perceived duty of care.[[15]](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/sahar-dahan_audit-readiness-security-readiness-they-activity-7436902548340396032-cMsL)
- **Indemnification and hold-harmless**: The vendor (or kolm) indemnifies the signer against claims arising from the attestation, except for gross negligence/willful misconduct. Hold-harmless letters for third-party contexts.[[10]](https://www.icaew.com/regulation/managing-professional-liability)
- **Insurance**: kolm/vendor provides or funds E&O/PLI naming the individual (or with adequate limits), plus possible cyber coverage. Limitation-of-liability clauses (caps on damages) where permitted.
- **Other**: Clear engagement letter defining scope, assumptions, methodology, and permitted use; possibly peer review or quality controls.

Note: Regulators (e.g., OCC interagency advisory) view certain liability limitations in audit/attestation engagements as unsafe/unsound and potentially impairing independence under AICPA/SEC/PCAOB rules.[[16]](https://www.occ.treas.gov/news-issuances/news-releases/2006/pub-interagency-advisory-2006-10a.pdf)[[16]](https://www.occ.treas.gov/news-issuances/news-releases/2006/pub-interagency-advisory-2006-10a.pdf) Formal attestations face stricter scrutiny than informal “readiness” reviews.

Practitioner sentiment (from discussions on pentests/reports) highlights risks of boilerplate or insufficiently rigorous work leading to client/investor reliance and later disputes.[[17]](https://x.com/hetmehtaa/status/2040881534522249490)

### Minimum Legal/Insurance Scaffolding kolm Must Offer
To recruit credible co-signers, kolm (the startup) should minimally provide (each point cited to sources above):

1. **Written engagement agreement** with explicit scope limits, “readiness attestation—not a certification/audit” language, disclaimers on reliance/guarantees, and permitted use only. (AICPA attestation standards; ICAEW liability management guidance.)[[10]](https://www.icaew.com/regulation/managing-professional-liability)[[18]](https://www.armanino.com/articles/aicpa-issues-clarified-attestation-standard-audit/)

2. **Indemnification clause** holding the signer harmless except for their own gross negligence/willful misconduct, plus possible hold-harmless for third parties. (ICAEW guidance on managing professional liability.)[[10]](https://www.icaew.com/regulation/managing-professional-liability)

3. **Adequate E&O/professional liability insurance** (e.g., $1–2M+ limits per claim/aggregate) specifically covering the attestation work, with the individual named or endorsed. (Standard expectations for auditors/vCISOs; vendor contract norms.)[[3]](https://www.vouch.us/blog/accounting-insurance)[[5]](https://www.risk.cornell.edu/vendor-provider-main-page/consultant-or-non-specialist/)

4. **Liability cap or limitation provisions** (where legally permissible and not impairing any independence analysis). (Common in professional engagements, subject to regulatory caveats.)[[16]](https://www.occ.treas.gov/news-issuances/news-releases/2006/pub-interagency-advisory-2006-10a.pdf)

5. **Clear distinction from formal standards** (no implication of SSAE 18, GAAS, etc., compliance) to frame it as advisory/limited-scope. (AICPA/attestation practice distinctions; “readiness vs. certification” framing.)[[15]](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/sahar-dahan_audit-readiness-security-readiness-they-activity-7436902548340396032-cMsL)

Additional helpful elements include kolm maintaining its own cyber/E&O coverage and offering transparent methodology/evidence for the signer’s review. Without this scaffolding, reputational and personal liability risks will deter qualified signers. Consult legal counsel specializing in professional liability for jurisdiction-specific advice, as rules vary (e.g., common-law third-party liability).
